title: Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita, 2023
Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and vegetable oil availability for 2023, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against vegetable oil supply in kilograms per year per capita on the vertical axis. The data points, color-coded by continent, show a general positive association where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher vegetable oil supply, though the distribution is wide and contains significant outliers. At the lower end of the economic spectrum, countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo and Niger cluster below $5,000 GDP per capita with oil supplies under 5 kg. In the middle range, nations such as Jordan, Belize, and Iraq occupy the $10,000 to $20,000 band with supplies between 10 kg and 15 kg. Several countries deviate from the central trend; Brazil and Algeria show supplies near 25 kg despite GDPs around $20,000, while Guinea-Bissau maintains a supply of 20 kg with a GDP under $5,000. Conversely, high-income nations display varied consumption levels: the United States and Canada sit near 28 kg, whereas Denmark and Norway fall below 10 kg despite GDPs exceeding $60,000. European countries generally cluster in the upper right quadrant with high GDP and moderate-to-high oil supply, while many African nations remain in the lower left. The data is expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The visible pattern shows that while wealthier nations typically have greater access to vegetable oils, specific national diets and agricultural outputs create substantial variation independent of income levels.
